texts.orig: In recent years, Li consumption has increased significantly because rechargeable Li batteries are widely used in portable electronic devices and  electric  vehicles  [1]. texts.orig: A B S T R A C T
texts.text: A B S T R A C T

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A  molecular  dynamics  simulation  was  performed  to  explore  the  diffusion  behavior  of  counter-ions  within polyelectrolyte  membranes  via  Materials  Studio  software,  which  could  provide  a  theoretical  basis  for  the screening  and  performance  prediction  of  selective  ion-exchange  membranes  (IEMs).  The  mean  square  displacements of Li þ /Mg 2 þ in sulfonic and phosphatic polyelectrolyte systems were calculated, and the sulfonic group was determined as a higher preference fixed charge group. The conformational variation of the polyelectrolyte chain with the concentration of counter-ion was assessed via the radius of gyration and, in combination  with  the  pair  correlations  function.  The  interaction  of  counter-ions  with  sulfonic  groups  in  cation exchange membranes (CEMs) was also determined. The results showed that Mg 2 þ ions were easily adsorbed on sulfonic groups in CEMs, shielding the negative charge of the polyelectrolyte, which resulted in the coiling of the molecular chain; hence, the radius of gyration decreased. By replacing the adsorbed Li þ ions, more Mg 2 þ ions were preferentially bound on the sulfonic groups with the increase in Mg 2 þ ions in the aqueous solution, which is consistent with the ion-exchange isotherms of the Selemion CSO membrane. By calculating the interaction energy of the counter-ions toward the polyelectrolyte and via a permeation experiment involving various cations, we found the difference in interaction energy between monovalent and divalent ions was remarkable, indicating that the interaction of counter-ions toward fixed charge groups in polyelectrolyte mainly determined the ionic diffusion performance. Raising the energy barrier on the interface was determined as an efficient method for the modification of selective IEMs.

In particular, it is important to separate Li þ from the  brine  with  a  high  concentration  of  Mg 2 þ because  it  has  similar chemical properties as those of Li and Mg. Several methods, such as adsorption  [5 -7],  extraction  [8 -10],  and  electrodialysis  (ED)  with ion-selective exchange membranes, have been developed to recover Li from brines with high Mg/Li ratios [11 -13]. As discussed in our previous study [11,12], ED using a batch mode of operation was performed to recover Li from brines with variable feed characteristics. In addition, the partitioning principle was explained via a thermodynamic analysis of the aqueous species distribution of ions and the ion-exchange membrane (IEM) properties.

texts.orig: The retardation of the molecular chain of the counterion in space, and the interaction between the intrinsically charged group and counterion determine the migration behavior of the counterion in the membrane. The diffusion process of counterions in polyelectrolyte molecule-water solution system is studied at the molecular/atomic scale. Exploring the selective mechanism of ion exchange membranes is critical. Compared with experimental research, molecular simulation has the advantages of low cost, less workload, and high efficiency. In recent years, many researchers  have  used  molecular  simulation  to  study  the  diffusion behavior  and  interaction  mechanisms  in  polymers  [14 -16].  Many experimental phenomena have been fully explained on the molecular scale [17 -19].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Stevens and Kremer performed groundbreaking work for molecular simulation  of  polyelectrolyte-water  solution  systems  with  ionizable groups  [20 -22].  By  investigating  the  polyelectrolyte-water  solution model  with  different  concentrations,  they  found  that  the  spatial configuration  of  the  polyelectrolyte  molecules  was  not  linear,  but curved, and that the counter ions would aggregate around the polyelectrolytes with the shrinkage of the molecular chains. Liu et al. studied the  aggregation  behavior  of  counterions  around  the  polyelectrolyte molecules, and discussed the effects of counter ion valency, molecular chain length, and other factors [23]. Konieczny et al. studied the molecular configurations of polyelectrolytes and the aggregation behavior of the surrounding counterions and found that some counterions were localized around the polyelectrolyte molecules, while other counterions were freely  dispersed  in  the  aqueous  phase  [24].  Molnar  et  al.  used all-atom  molecular  dynamics  simulation  (MDS)  to  investigate  the interaction between Ca 2 þ and sodium polyacrylate molecules [25]. They observed that Ca 2 þ significantly affected the distribution of Na þ around the  polyelectrolyte  molecules,  and  Ca 2 þ could  effectively  shield  the inherent negative charge on the polyelectrolyte molecular chain and promote the shrinkage of the molecular chain. Huang et al. discussed the interaction between Ca 2 þ ,  Na þ ,  H2O, and polymethacrylic acid molecules [26,27]. They found that the geometric size (radius of gyration) of the  polyelectrolyte  molecule  first  decreased  and  thereafter  increased with the increase of Ca 2 þ concentration. The intrinsic mechanism was explained by the interaction between counterions, water molecules, and carboxylic acid groups on the polyelectrolyte chain. However, no study on the diffusion behavior of different ions in the brine and the interaction mechanism in polymers has yet been reported.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this study, the polyelectrolyte chain-counter ion aqueous solution model was established, and the diffusion process of Li þ and Mg 2 þ ions in the membrane was simulated by MDS. The mechanism of interaction between the polyelectrolyte chain and counterions was first studied to investigate  the  diffusion  properties  of  the  polyelectrolyte  membrane material  with  different  fixed  charged  groups.  The  simulation  results were  experimentally  verified.  The  simulation  study  can  provide  a theoretical  basis  for  the  screening  and  performance  prediction  of  selective ion-exchange membranes.

texts.orig: 2.1.1. Molecular force field selection
texts.text: 2.1.1. Molecular force field selection

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  simulation  system  studied  in  this  paper  contained  the  polyelectrolyte molecules, metal cations (Li þ , Na þ , K þ , Mg 2 þ , and Ca 2 þ ), and water molecules. It represented the organic-inorganic hybrid system, and the COMPASS force field could provide the precise atomic parameters for polyelectrolyte molecules, inorganic ions, and water molecules. Prior studies have demonstrated the applicability of the COMPASS force field to such organic-inorganic hybrid systems [28,29].
texts.text: The  simulation  system  studied  in  this  paper  contained  the  polyelectrolyte molecules, metal cations (Li þ , Na þ , K þ , Mg 2 þ , and Ca 2 þ ), and water molecules. It represented the organic-inorganic hybrid system, and the COMPASS force field could provide the precise atomic parameters for polyelectrolyte molecules, inorganic ions, and water molecules. Prior studies have demonstrated the applicability of the COMPASS force field to such organic-inorganic hybrid systems [28,29].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The newly added COMPASSII force field in Materials Studio software enhances the support of polymers and heterocyclic systems on the basis of the COMPASS force field. Therefore, the COMPASSII force field was selected for MDS in this study.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.1.2. Model building
texts.text: 2.1.2. Model building

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 2.1.2.1. Construction of the molecular model of polyelectrolyte chain. The chemical composition of a selective cation exchange membrane (CEM) is typically  a  strong  acid-type  aromatic  polyelectrolyte  chain;  it can  be classified into a sulfonic acid-type and a phosphoric acid-type according to the fixed charge functional group. Utilizing a sulfonic acid-type CEM as an example, the construction process of the polyelectrolyte molecular chain was as follows. The initial configuration of the polyelectrolyte molecular  chain  was  completed  using  the  Build  Polymers  toolbar  in Materials  Studio  software.  First,  the  monomer  structures  of  ethylbenzene sulfonic acid (denoted by A) and p-diethylbenzene (denoted by B) were constructed using the Sketch toolbar as the repeat unit in the first step (Fig. 1). The first and last atoms of monomers A and B were specified  for  condensation,  and  the  copolymer  A-B  with  a  degree  of polymerization of 1 was constructed using the Block Copolymer command under the Build Polymers toolbar, as shown in Fig. 2. Then, the copolymer A-B was used as the monomer to construct the homopolymer -[A-B]-n with the degree of polymerization, n, using the homopolymer command in the Build Polymers toolbar. The initial configuration of a polyelectrolyte chain with the degree of polymerization, n, was constructed (Fig. 3). To enable the molecular simulation to have a reference value for experimental research and reduce the simulation error, the degree of polymerization, n, of the polyelectrolyte molecular chain -[AB]-n was set as 20. The COMPASSII force field was selected under the Forcite module to assign a force field to all atoms in the polyelectrolyte chain and geometrically optimize it.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 2.1.2.2. Construction  of  water  molecule  model. The  COMPASSII  selfcontained force field parameters were used in the simulation to fit the water molecular model, and a planar 3-point SPC-like rigid body water molecular model [30] was obtained. Among these, the force field type for a H atom was: h1o (hydrogen, bonded to O, F), and the force field type for an O atom was: o2* (oxygen, SP3, in water). The Forcite module was used for geometry optimization of water molecules.

The Forcite module was used for geometry optimization of water molecules.

texts.orig: 2.1.2.3. Construction of inorganic ion model. Several 3D atomistic files were created and Li, Mg, Na, K, Ca, and Cl atoms were built separately in the simulation, which assigned the charge to each atom on the Charge panel of the Properties list (Li: þ 1, Mg: þ 2, K: þ 1, Na: þ 1, Cl:-1), and used the Forcite module to assign a suitable force field type to all atoms. The force field type for the Li atom was: Li þ (lithium, þ 1 ion), the force field type for a Mg atom was: Mg 2 þ (magnesium, þ 2 ion), the force field type for the Na atom was: Na þ (sodium, þ 1 ion), the force field type used by K atom was: K þ (potassium, þ 1 ion), the force field type for Ca atom was: Ca 2 þ (calcium, þ 2 ion), and the force field type for Cl atom was: Cl (chlorine, -1 anion).
texts.text: 2.1.2.3. Construction of inorganic ion model. Several 3D atomistic files were created and Li, Mg, Na, K, Ca, and Cl atoms were built separately in the simulation, which assigned the charge to each atom on the Charge panel of the Properties list (Li: þ 1, Mg: þ 2, K: þ 1, Na: þ 1, Cl:-1), and used the Forcite module to assign a suitable force field type to all atoms. The force field type for the Li atom was: Li þ (lithium, þ 1 ion), the force field type for a Mg atom was: Mg 2 þ (magnesium, þ 2 ion), the force field type for the Na atom was: Na þ (sodium, þ 1 ion), the force field type used by K atom was: K þ (potassium, þ 1 ion), the force field type for Ca atom was: Ca 2 þ (calcium, þ 2 ion), and the force field type for Cl atom was: Cl (chlorine, -1 anion).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 2.1.2.4. Model  construction  of  polyelectrolyte  aqueous  solution  system. Amorphous cell module was used to build the simulation box. Using the polyelectrolyte chain -MgCl2 aqueous solution system as an example, a cube with periodic boundary conditions was established. According to the measured wet density of the CEM, the system density was 1.05 g/ cm 3 , the cube edge length was maintained at approximately 30 Å, which changed slightly with the number of inorganic atoms in the simulation system. The temperature was set to be constant at 298 K, and the highest convergence accuracy was selected (Ultra-fine). This cube consisted of a polyelectrolyte molecular chain with a degree of polymerization of 20, 500 real water molecules, certain number of Mg 2 þ ions, and balanced system charge of Cl ions. The system maintained electrical neutrality, as shown in Fig. 4, where the purple, red, yellow, white, dark green, and light  green parts represent the backbone chain of the polyelectrolyte molecules,  O  atoms,  S  atoms,  H  atoms,  Mg 2 þ ions,  and  Cl ions, respectively. After the AC simulation box was built, the Forcite module was used for geometry optimization .

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.1.3. Simulation calculation
texts.text: 2.1.3. Simulation calculation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: MDSs were performed using the Forcite module in Materials Studio software.  First,  the  Conjugate  gradient  was  chosen  to  geometrically optimize the initial structure of the system. Next, dynamic equilibrium (Dynamics)  was  performed  on  the  NPT  ensemble  ( P ¼ 0.0001  Gpa, T ¼ 298 K) to obtain a stable system density with a simulation time of 500 ps. For the last frame structure obtained by the NPT ensemble under the equilibrium process, the molecules of the polyelectrolyte chain were fixed using Constraints under the Modify toolbar, and the ions to be analyzed were designated as set, followed by the NVT ensemble (regular ensemble) for dynamic equilibrium. The total simulation time under the NVT ensemble was 1.5 ns: an initial 0.5 ns for system balance and 1 ns for system sampling analysis (Table 1 includes the simulation parameters). In all dynamic simulations, the initial velocity of the atoms in the system was randomly assigned by the Boltzmann distribution at a given temperature; the non-bonded interaction energy had a truncation radius of 18.5 Å, and the summation method was Atom Based. The method of summing up the coulomb electrostatic potential energy was ParticleParticle-Particle Mesh (PPPM), which could increase the speed of the large  system  Ewald  and  improve  the  computational  efficiency  when dealing  with  large-scale  charged  systems.  The  Berendsen  constant temperature  thermal  bath  method  was  selected  for  the  temperature control, and the Berendsen constant pressure device was selected for the pressure control. The simulated time step was 1.0 fs, and the trace file recording  period  was  5000  steps.  To  confirm  if  the  equilibrium  was reached, the total energy of the molecular dynamics system under the
texts.text: MDSs were performed using the Forcite module in Materials Studio software.  First,  the  Conjugate  gradient  was  chosen  to  geometrically optimize the initial structure of the system. Next, dynamic equilibrium (Dynamics)  was  performed  on  the  NPT  ensemble  ( P ¼ 0.0001  Gpa, T ¼ 298 K) to obtain a stable system density with a simulation time of 500 ps. For the last frame structure obtained by the NPT ensemble under the equilibrium process, the molecules of the polyelectrolyte chain were fixed using Constraints under the Modify toolbar, and the ions to be analyzed were designated as set, followed by the NVT ensemble (regular ensemble) for dynamic equilibrium. The total simulation time under the NVT ensemble was 1.5 ns: an initial 0.5 ns for system balance and 1 ns for system sampling analysis (Table 1 includes the simulation parameters). In all dynamic simulations, the initial velocity of the atoms in the system was randomly assigned by the Boltzmann distribution at a given temperature; the non-bonded interaction energy had a truncation radius of 18.5 Å, and the summation method was Atom Based. The method of summing up the coulomb electrostatic potential energy was ParticleParticle-Particle Mesh (PPPM), which could increase the speed of the large  system  Ewald  and  improve  the  computational  efficiency  when dealing  with  large-scale  charged  systems.  The  Berendsen  constant temperature  thermal  bath  method  was  selected  for  the  temperature control, and the Berendsen constant pressure device was selected for the pressure control. The simulated time step was 1.0 fs, and the trace file recording  period  was  5000  steps. texts.orig: 2.2.1. Ion exchange isotherm
texts.text: 2.2.1. Ion exchange isotherm

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The ion exchange isotherm of the CSO membrane was measured to investigate the difference in the interaction ability of the sulfonic acidtype  polyelectrolyte  chain  to  Li þ /Mg 2 þ ,  and  to  verify  the  molecular simulation results. A mixed solution of monovalent and divalent salts with identical Cl-ion concentration was used to balance the CSO CEMs used in the experiments. By soaking the membrane samples in a binary mixture solution with a constant total cation equivalent concentration and  different  concentration  ratio,  when  the  ion  exchange  reached equilibrium, the concentrations of the two cations in the membrane and in the solution were determined, and the mole fraction was calculated. The ion exchange isotherm was obtained based on the measurement result of the equilibrium state.
texts.text: The ion exchange isotherm of the CSO membrane was measured to investigate the difference in the interaction ability of the sulfonic acidtype  polyelectrolyte  chain  to  Li þ /Mg 2 þ ,  and  to  verify  the  molecular simulation results. A mixed solution of monovalent and divalent salts with identical Cl-ion concentration was used to balance the CSO CEMs used in the experiments. By soaking the membrane samples in a binary mixture solution with a constant total cation equivalent concentration and  different  concentration  ratio,  when  the  ion  exchange  reached equilibrium, the concentrations of the two cations in the membrane and in the solution were determined, and the mole fraction was calculated. The ion exchange isotherm was obtained based on the measurement result of the equilibrium state.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The specific measurement steps were as follows. (1) The CSO CEM sample (2 � 2 cm 2 ) was soaked with deionized water for several days; (2) the ion exchange membrane sample was placed in 0.1 mol/L LiCl solution, and was equilibrated for more than 24 h at 25 � C to reduce the adsorption amount of the electrolyte on the ion exchange membrane sample, and to prevent the cations in the subsequent monovalent and bivalent mixed solution system from being adsorbed and depleted; (3) the  ion  exchange  membrane  sample  was  equilibrated  with  50 mL  of LiCl -MgCl2  solution  with  a  total  cation  equivalent  concentration  of 0.1 mol/L at 25 � C for 24 h; utilizing binary system equilibrium solutions with different Li þ /Mg 2 þ concentration ratios, a series of experimental points were obtained. ICP-OES was used to measure the concentration of Li þ and Mg 2 þ ions in the equilibrium solution to calculate the cation composition in the equilibrium liquid phase system. (4) After immersing the sample in 50 mL of 1 mol/L HCl solution at 25 � C for 24 h, the cation adsorbed  on  the  ion  exchange  membrane  sample  was  completely exchanged by H þ ions, and the concentration of Li þ and Mg 2 þ ions was measured by ICP-OES to calculate the cation composition in the membrane at equilibrium.
texts.text: The specific measurement steps were as follows. (1) The CSO CEM sample (2 � 2 cm 2 ) was soaked with deionized water for several days; (2) the ion exchange membrane sample was placed in 0.1 mol/L LiCl solution, and was equilibrated for more than 24 h at 25 � C to reduce the adsorption amount of the electrolyte on the ion exchange membrane sample, and to prevent the cations in the subsequent monovalent and bivalent mixed solution system from being adsorbed and depleted; (3) the  ion  exchange  membrane  sample  was  equilibrated  with  50 mL  of LiCl -MgCl2  solution  with  a  total  cation  equivalent  concentration  of 0.1 mol/L at 25 � C for 24 h; utilizing binary system equilibrium solutions with different Li þ /Mg 2 þ concentration ratios, a series of experimental points were obtained. ICP-OES was used to measure the concentration of Li þ and Mg 2 þ ions in the equilibrium solution to calculate the cation composition in the equilibrium liquid phase system. (4) After immersing the sample in 50 mL of 1 mol/L HCl solution at 25 � C for 24 h, the cation adsorbed  on  the  ion  exchange  membrane  sample  was  completely exchanged by H þ ions, and the concentration of Li þ and Mg 2 þ ions was measured by ICP-OES to calculate the cation composition in the membrane at equilibrium.

texts.orig: 3.1.1.1. Effect  of  fixed  charge  functional  groups. The chemical composition of CEMs is typically a strong acid-type aromatic polyelectrolyte chain with fixed charge groups. In this study, MDS was carried out by designing the types of charged groups. The diffusion properties of cations  with  different  valence  states  were  investigated  to  study  the migration  selectivity  of  cations  in  the  system.  Polyelectrolyte  chains with the monomer, - A - B -, and degree of polymerization of 20 were constructed by using sulfonic and phosphoric acid groups as fixed charge groups  of  polyelectrolyte  chains.  Li þ and Mg 2 þ were selected  as  the research objects to establish simulation boxes. The simulation system consisted of a polyelectrolyte chain, 500H2O molecules, one cation (Li þ or Mg 2 þ ) and Cl ions to balance the charge. The molecular dynamics calculations  were  carried  out.  The  MSD  of  Li þ and  Mg 2 þ ions  was determined to investigate the  diffusion  behavior  of  different  valence ions in the system.

It can be observed from Fig. 6 that in the sulfonic acid-type polyelectrolyte-water solution system, the MSD of Li þ ions linearly increases with time, and the MSD of Mg 2 þ ions appears to be constant at zero. This phenomenon can be attributed to the localization of ions in the system, typically because the ions in the system are confined to the vibration in the channel, and no obvious displacement occurs. The Mg 2 þ ions in the Salt lake  brine  used  for  Li  extraction  are  mostly  close  to  saturation.  The numbers of Mg 2 þ ions are much higher than that of Li þ ions that can be used for migration. It is difficult and uneconomical to migrate all Mg 2 þ ions. Therefore, it is disadvantageous to separate Li þ ions from the high Mg/Li ratio Salt lake brine system. Compared with the sulfonic acid-type polyelectrolyte chain, the diffusion retardation effect of the phosphoric acid-type polyelectrolyte chain on the cation is very large, such that the MSD is remarkably reduced, and the diffusion rate of ions is weakened. Therefore, for the extraction of Li from Salt lake brine with high Mg/Li ratio,  the  sulfonic  acid  group  is  an  ideal  fixed  charge  group  for  a monovalent selective polyelectrolyte ion exchange membrane.

texts.text: 3.1.2. Interaction between polyelectrolyte chain and Li þ /Mg 2 þ

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The interaction mechanism between the polyelectrolyte chain and Li þ /Mg 2 þ was further explored to determine the decisive influencing factors of ion diffusion behavior. First, the Li þ /Mg 2 þ two-component counterion-polyelectrolyte  chain-aqueous  solution  simulation  system was established for MDS. The simulation system consisted of 5 polyelectrolyte chains, and 2500H2O molecules. The number of Li þ ions in the fixed system was 50, and the Mg/Li ratio was adjusted by changing the amount of Mg 2 þ ions in the simulated box. The amount of Cl ions was obtained according  to  the  charge  balance,  and  other  simulation details were as described above. Fig. 13 shows the two models of the molecular configuration of the polyelectrolyte chain as a function of the number of Mg 2 þ ions in the system.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 14 reflects the distribution of Mg 2 þ ions around the sulfonic acid O atom of the polyelectrolyte chain. There are two obvious peaks in the O -Mg distribution curve, which means there are two interaction layers. It is similar to the concept of ionic hydration shells of Mg 2 þ and Li þ ions [32,33]. The strongest peak appears at approximately 1.65 Å, indicating that under strong electrostatic attraction, the high probability of Mg 2 þ ions in the solution appears around the sulfonic acid group. The second peak of the O -Mg distribution curve appears at about 3.89 Å. Two Mg 2 þ ions positive-adsorption layers are formed near the positions of 1.65 and 3.89 Å  from  the  sulfonic  acid  O  atom.  The  first  peak  intensity  is approximately 60, and the peak is high and sharp, indicating that the interaction between Mg 2 þ ions and sulfonic acid groups is strong, and there is a close-range order, i.e., the relationship between O -Mg is tight. The second peak is low and broad, indicating that the interaction between Mg 2 þ ions and sulfonic acid groups is weak. With the increase in Mg 2 þ ions, the peaks ' intensity is slightly decreased. This is due to the excessive Mg 2 þ ions, resulting in the local enrichment of Mg 2 þ ions near the sulfonic acid adsorption site, which shields the negative charge of the sulfonic acid group and increases the local positive charge density of the adsorbed layer, such that the interaction between the Mg 2 þ ion and the sulfonic acid group is weakened.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 15 shows the distribution of Li þ ions around the sulfonic acid O atom of the polyelectrolyte chain. The phenomena are similar to those of Mg 2 þ ions. There are two obvious peaks in the O -Li distribution curve. The strongest peak of the O -Li distribution curve also appears at around 1.65 Å, and the second peak appears at about 3.89 Å. When the amount of Mg 2 þ ions in the system is 50, the strongest peak intensity of the O -Li distribution can reach approximately 49, because the number of sulfonic acid adsorption sites in the system exceeds the total number of cations, and the competitive adsorption effect between Li þ and Mg 2 þ ions is not significant.  As  the  number  of  Mg 2 þ ions  increases  to  100,  the  peak intensity of the O -Li distribution decreases significantly. This is because the interaction between Mg 2 þ ions and sulfonic acid groups is stronger. As the number of Mg 2 þ ions in the system increases, more Mg 2 þ ions enter  the  positive  electro-adsorption  layer  around  the  sulfonic  acid group and replace a considerable portion of Li þ ions and absorb with the sulfonic acid group, shielding the electrostatic attraction between the Li þ ion  and  the  sulfonic  acid  group,  which  results  in  the  stronger diffusion ability of Li þ ion in the system. By studying the relationship between  the  correlation  function  of  O -Mg  and  O -Li  with  the  Mg 2 þ content, it can be predicted that by increasing the Mg/Li ratio can help in improving the Li þ /Mg 2 þ selectivity.

texts.text: 3.1.3. Permeation ability of associated cations

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Li þ , Mg 2 þ , Na þ , and K þ ions were considered as the research models to establish the simulation box to explore the migration order of Li þ associated cations in the sulfonic acid-type polyelectrolyte chain system. The polyelectrolyte chain-aqueous solution simulation system contained 5 polyelectrolyte chains, 2500H2O molecules, 50 cations (Li þ ,  Mg 2 þ , Na þ , K þ , Ca 2 þ ) and Cl ions to balance the charge with charge balance, to perform molecular dynamics calculations. The molecular dynamics equilibrium  of  NVT  ensemble  was  analyzed  by  MSD,  and  the  MSD-t curves of different cations were constructed to compare the diffusion ability. The last frame of the molecular dynamics equilibrium under the NVT ensemble was considered and the spatial position constraints of the polyelectrolyte molecular chain were removed in the Modify toolbar. The total energy E Total, total cationic energy E A, and total energy E B  of the system, except cations, were calculated by Forcite module, and Δ E was calculated by formula (3), which was the interaction energy between the cation and the system.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 17 shows the interaction energy between different cations and the simulated system, which means this energy contains the interaction energy of ions-water and ions-polyelectrolyte. The order of interaction energy is: Mg 2 þ > Ca 2 þ > Li þ > Na þ > K þ . The interaction energy of divalent ions is significantly bigger than that of the monovalent ions. The higher interaction energy implies that the cation and polyelectrolyte molecules have higher energy barriers at the interface, and the interaction energy is the direct cause of the difference in the diffusion ability of each ion. The simulation results of molecular dynamics equilibrium and the experimental results of ion exchange isotherm measurements are also verified.

texts.orig: The ion exchange isotherm of the CSO CEM in the LiCl -MgCl2 binary system is shown in Fig. 18. The curve is bent to the lower right of the diagonal line, indicating that the CSO CEM preferentially adsorbs the divalent ion Mg 2 þ . Only when the monovalent ion Li þ content in the binary solution system is dominant (close to 1.0), the adsorption ratio of the CEM to the divalent ion increases significantly. Fig. 19 shows the ion exchange capacity and the exchange amount of each antiparticle with the liquid phase composition of Li þ solution. The ion exchange capacity of the membrane remains relatively stable. However, the ion exchange capacity  of  Mg 2 þ is  always  significantly  greater  than  that  of  Li þ regardless of the increase of Li þ equivalent fraction, and the difference only slightly decreases with the increase of Li þ content. In summary, the ability of the sulfonic acid-type CEM to bind divalent ions is significantly stronger than that of the monovalent ions, which verifies the simulation conclusion.

For  isovalent  cations,  the  difference  in  the energy  barrier  of  the  contact  surface  is  not  significant,  as  shown  in Fig. 20. texts.orig: Both simulation and  experimental results  show  that  the  diffusion behavior between the monovalent and divalent counterions is significantly  different  in  the  presence  of  sulfonic  acid-type  polyelectrolyte molecules. The interaction between the fixed charge functional group of the polyelectrolyte molecule and the diffused counterion determines the energy barrier at the diffusion interface, which in turn significantly affects the retardation of different counterions through the membrane. The effect of migration selectivity is determined by the properties of the membrane materials.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The molecular dynamics equilibrium calculation was used to simulate the diffusion process of the major cations in the brine system, and the charged functional groups of ion exchange membranes suitable for separation of Li and Mg were screened. The screening results show that the sulfonic acid group is an ideal fixed-charge group for the monovalent selective  ion  exchange  membrane,  and  the  phosphate-based  ion  exchange membrane does not have potential to separate Li from the brine with high concentration of Mg.
